engineering.translation: The childish (bāla) — not the wise (paṇḍita) — pronounce sāṅkhya (knowledge-path) and yoga (action-path) as separate. The one who is established in even one of them properly obtains the fruit of both. Krishna names the false dichotomy directly: those who think the two paths are different are not the wise. The wise know they reach the same end. The engineering analog: the engineer who insists IC and management are categorically different vocations, that hands-on coding and architectural strategy are unbridgeable, that build and mentor are zero-sum. That developer is not the senior the verse calls paṇḍita. The principal who has deeply internalised one of the modes finds the other already present in it. The IC who has built mastery in shipping discovers architectural insight emerges from it. The architect who has built mastery in design discovers shipping discipline emerges from it.
engineering.concrete_scenario: An engineering org has a recurring debate during all-hands. The IC track and the management track, the team says, require fundamentally different skills; promotion paths are separate; cross-track movement is rare. A staff engineer who has been on both tracks across her career listens to the debate and pushes back: 'When I led the migration group for two years as an engg manager, I made better Postgres index decisions than I ever made as a pure IC. I had absorbed how index choices propagated through team morale and on-call rotation. When I came back to IC and built the Kafka pipeline, I shipped a cleaner design than I would have without the manager years. I had absorbed how interfaces affect downstream-squad velocity.' The two paths produced the same fruit through different surfaces. The team's persistent dichotomising is, the verse names, the position of the bāla. The cross-reference inventory: Redis, MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, Jenkins, CircleCI.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader concludes the two paths are operationally identical. Day-to-day, the IC and the manager do different things; the architect and the builder use different muscles. The verse does not deny operational difference; it denies that the operational difference produces a difference of fruit at the level the verse cares about.
engineering.counter_example: When an engineer is genuinely better suited to one path than the other — when the manager-track person is miserable in IC roles, or the IC-track person is dysfunctional in management — the verse does not require either to claim the other. The verse names what the paṇḍita knows; it does not legislate that every engineer must travel both paths.
engineering.implication: The next time someone on your team frames IC-vs-management or hands-on-vs-strategic as zero-sum, name the framing as bāla per Krishna. The wise see the paths as reaching the same fruit through different surfaces.
engineering.quotable_line: The engineer who insists IC and management are categorically different is not the senior the verse calls wise.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 5.4 engineering layer drafted from triangulated source pack. Anchored to chapter-5 thesis: equivalence-of-paths-with-karma-yoga-preference, decoupled identity, sama-darśana, internal-bliss-source, brahma-nirvāṇa-as-operational-rung-with-honest-STRETCHED. Cross-references to chapters 2-4 preserved.
